questions.text: The direction of the magnetic lines of force can be found by using:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The right-hand rule helps determine the direction of the magnetic field around a current-carrying wire. By pointing your thumb in the direction of the current, your curled fingers indicate the circular direction of the magnetic field lines.
Henry's law relates to gas solubility in liquids and is unrelated to electromagnetism or the direction of magnetic fields.
The left-hand rule is used for determining the direction of force on a conductor in a magnetic field, not the direction of magnetic field lines due to current.
Faraday's law explains electromagnetic induction and how a changing magnetic field can induce an electric current. It does not describe the direction of magnetic field lines.

questions.explanation: The right-hand rule is specifically designed to find the direction of the magnetic field created by a current-carrying wire. By pointing your thumb in the direction of the current flow, your fingers naturally curl in the direction of the magnetic field lines. This is different from Henry's law, which pertains to gas solubility, and Faraday's law, which discusses electromagnetic induction. Similarly, the left-hand rule is used to determine the direction of force in a magnetic field, not the magnetic field itself.

questions.text: Magnetic field will not produce in case of?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because A positive charge, top, moving perpendicularly through a magnetic field is deflected. The right-hand rule, bottom, predicts the direction in which the positive charge will be deflected. A similar left-hand rule predicts the deflection of negative charges.

This option is incorrect because A negative charge introduces a negative sign, which flips the direction of the force. So, for a negative charge your right hand lies to you, and the force on the negative charge will be opposite to the direction indicated by your right hand.

This option is correct because Magnetic fields will not be produced in case of neutral particles. The protons, cathode rays and alpha particles are charged particles, so the magnetic field deflects them. But there is no charge on the neutrons, so they are not deflected by the magnetic field.
This option is incorrect because Option C is correct.

questions.explanation: Correct option is C. Magnetic fields will not be produced in case of neutral particles. The protons, cathode rays and alpha particles are charged particles, so the magnetic field deflects them. But there is no charge on the neutrons, so they are not deflected by the magnetic field.

questions.text: How can a magnetic field be produced?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A permanent magnet has a persistent magnetic field due to the alignment of magnetic domains within the material.
An electric current flowing through a conductor generates a magnetic field around it, as described by Ampère's law.
A temporary magnet, such as an electromagnet, can produce a magnetic field when an electric current is applied, but not when the current is off.

options.text: Using a permanent magnet or electric current
options.explanation: Both permanent magnets and electric currents can produce magnetic fields. Permanent magnets have a constant field, whereas electric currents produce a field when flowing through a conductor.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D: Using a permanent magnet or electric current. Permanent magnets inherently have a magnetic field due to the alignment of magnetic domains, while electric currents generate a magnetic field around the conductor through which they flow. This is consistent with the principles of electromagnetism. Option A is incorrect because while permanent magnets do produce magnetic fields, this option does not account for the role of electric currents. Option B is incorrect as it only considers electric currents and not permanent magnets. Option C is misleading as temporary magnets do produce magnetic fields, but they require an electric current to do so, which is not mentioned in the option.

questions.text: Electric charge in uniform motion produces:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: An electric field is a region of space around an electric charge where other charges experience a force. The strength of the electric field is proportional to the magnitude of the charge and inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the charge.

A magnetic field is a region of space around a magnet or a moving electric charge where other magnets or moving electric charges experience a force. The strength of the magnetic field is proportional to the strength of the magnet or the current in the wire and inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the magnet or wire.

When an electric charge is in uniform motion, it produces both an electric field and a magnetic field. This is known as electromagnetic radiation.Electric field: An electric field is a region of space around an electric charge where other charges experience a force. The strength of the electric field is proportional to the magnitude of the charge and inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the charge.Magnetic field: A magnetic field is a region of space around a magnet or a moving electric charge where other magnets or moving electric charges experience a force. The strength of the magnetic field is proportional to the strength of the magnet or the current in the wire and inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the magnet or wire.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: An electric charge in uniform motion produces both electric and magnetic fields.A stationary electric charge produces only an electric field. However, a moving electric charge produces both an electric field and a magnetic field. The electric field is produced by the charge's static electric field, while the magnetic field is produced by the charge's moving electric field.The strength of the electric field produced by a moving charge is proportional to the charge's magnitude and its speed. The direction of the electric field is perpendicular to the direction of the charge's motion.The strength of the magnetic field produced by a moving charge is proportional to the charge's magnitude, its speed, and its perpendicular distance from the field point. The direction of the magnetic field is perpendicular to both the direction of the charge's motion and the direction from the charge to the field point.The following are some examples of electric charges in uniform motion that produce both electric and magnetic fields:A charged particle moving through a vacuumA current-carrying wireA rotating magnet

questions.text: When currents are moving in the same direction in two conductors, the force will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The force between two conductors with currents flowing in the same direction is attraction.This is because the magnetic fields produced by the two currents are aligned in the same direction. When two magnetic fields are aligned in the same direction, they attract each other.The force of attraction between two conductors with currents flowing in the same direction is given by the following equation:F = μ0I1I2 / 2πrwhere:F is the force of attractionμ0 is the permeability of free spaceI1 and I2 are the currents in the two conductorsr is the distance between the conductors

Repulsive: This is the force that would occur if the currents were flowing in opposite directions. The magnetic fields produced by the two currents would be aligned in opposite directions, and when two magnetic fields are aligned in opposite directions, they repel each other.

Retracting: This is a type of repulsive force that would occur if the currents were flowing in opposite directions and the conductors were close together. The magnetic fields produced by the two currents would create a force that would tend to pull the conductors apart.

Opposing: This is a type of repulsive force that would occur if the currents were flowing in opposite directions and the conductors were far apart. The magnetic fields produced by the two currents would create a force that would tend to slow down the currents.

questions.explanation: The force between two conductors with currents flowing in the same direction is attraction. This is because the magnetic fields produced by the two currents are aligned in the same direction. When two magnetic fields are aligned in the same direction, they attract each other.The force of attraction between two conductors with currents flowing in the same direction is given by the following equation:F = μ0I1I2 / 2πrwhere:F is the force of attractionμ0 is the permeability of free spaceI1 and I2 are the currents in the two conductorsr is the distance between the conductors

According to Formula of Magnetic Force between two wires,F= I1I2L2dF I1 I2 l F 12dF is directly proportional to Permeability, Current of two wires and length.F is inversely proportional to 2 and distance.

According to Formula of Magnetic Force between two wires,F= I1I2L2dF I1 I2 l F 12dF is directly proportional to Permeability, Current of two wires and length.F is inversely proportional to 2 and distance.

questions.text: Two long, parallel conductors which are free to move are arranged 1.0 cm apart. A steady current of 20 A flows in each of the conductors in the same direction. The conductors:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Remains stationary: This would only be true if there was no current or if the currents were flowing in opposite directions.
When two parallel wires carry current in the same direction, they exert equal and opposite attractive forces on each other. This causes them to move towards one another.
Move away from each other: This would happen if the currents were flowing in opposite directions, creating a repulsive force.

options.text: Move at right angles to each other
options.explanation: Move at right angles to each other: The force between the conductors is either attractive or repulsive, acting along the line connecting them, not at right angles.

questions.explanation: When two parallel wires carry current in the same direction, they exert equal and opposite attractive forces on each other. This causes them to move towards one another.

questions.text: A long straight current-carrying conductor has current directed from bottom to top when held vertically. What will be the direction of magnetic field lines when observed from below the conductor?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. According to the right-hand rule, if you position your thumb in the direction of the current (upwards), your fingers will curl in a clockwise direction when viewed from below.
This option is incorrect. If the current flows from bottom to top, the magnetic field lines do not curl in an anti-clockwise direction when viewed from below.
This option is incorrect. The magnetic field lines do not travel vertically upward; instead, they circulate around the wire.
This option is incorrect. The magnetic field lines do not move directly downward; they are oriented in a circular pattern around the wire.

questions.explanation: The magnetic field generated by a long straight current-carrying conductor can be determined using the right-hand rule. When the current flows from bottom to top, if you point your right thumb in the direction of the current, your fingers will curl around the wire. When viewed from below, the fingers will curl in a clockwise direction, indicating that the magnetic field lines are oriented clockwise. The other options are incorrect because they do not represent the actual behavior of magnetic field lines around a straight current-carrying wire.
hints: current directed from bottom to top

questions.text: Two parallel straight conductors, in which current is flowing in the same direction, attract each other. The cause of it is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Two parallel straight conductors carrying current in the same direction attract each other due to the magnetic force between them. This happens because:Magnetic Fields Around the Conductors:Each current-carrying conductor generates a circular magnetic field around it, as described by Ampère's right-hand rule.Magnetic Interaction:The magnetic field of one conductor exerts a force on the second conductor and vice versa.When currents are in the same direction, the magnetic fields between the wires reinforce each other in a way that causes an attractive force.Lorentz Force Explanation:The moving charges (current) in one conductor experience a force due to the magnetic field created by the other conductor.According to the Lorentz force law, this force acts inwards, pulling the conductors together.
Two parallel straight conductors carrying current in the same direction attract each other due to the magnetic force between them.
Two parallel straight conductors carrying current in the same direction attract each other due to the magnetic force between them.
Two parallel straight conductors carrying current in the same direction attract each other due to the magnetic force between them.

questions.explanation: Correct option is 'A'.

questions.text: A unit of induction is said to exist at a point where the force per unit charge experienced by a positive test charge, moving with a velocity of 1ms-1 in the direction perpendicular to the field is 1 Newton. What is this phenomenon called?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A gravitational field is a region of space around a mass where another mass experiences a force of attraction. It is unrelated to the force experienced by a charge moving perpendicularly to a magnetic field.

The magnetic field is a region around a magnetic material or a moving electric charge within which the force of magnetism acts. A magnetic field is a force field that is created by moving electric charges and magnetic dipoles and exerts a force on other nearby moving charges and magnetic dipoles.
Magnetic flux is the measure of the number of magnetic field lines passing through a surface, not directly related to the force experienced by a moving charge.
Flux generally refers to the rate of flow of a property per unit area, and is not specific to the magnetic force experienced by a moving charge.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Magnetic Field. A magnetic field is defined by the force it exerts on moving charges. In this question, the force per unit charge experienced by a test charge moving perpendicularly to the field indicates the presence of a magnetic field. The force is proportional to the velocity of the charge and the strength of the magnetic field.Option A (Gravitational Field) is incorrect because it deals with forces due to gravity, not magnetic forces.Option C (Magnetic Flux) is incorrect because it describes the number of magnetic field lines passing through a surface, not the force on a moving charge.Option D (Flux) is too general and does not specifically relate to magnetic fields affecting moving charges.

questions.text: The direction of the magnetic line of force depends upon:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Nature of the material of the conducting wire
options.explanation: The direction of the magnetic lines of force does not depend upon the nature of the material of the conduction wire.
The direction of the magnetic lines of force does not depend upon the Area of the conducting wire.
The direction of the magnetic lines of force does not depend upon the Amount of the current.
The direction of the magnetic force depends on the direction of the current and the direction of the magnetic field.

questions.explanation: The direction of the magnetic force depends on the direction of the current and the direction of the magnetic field. Of these two, the direction of the current is in options so that will be the correct answer.

options.explanation: This figure represents the electric field of a positive charge.
Figure B represents the electric field of a negative charge.
This shows the direction of the magnetic field around a wire carrying current directed into the page.
This represents the magnetic field around a current carrying wire with current being directed out of the page.
Magnetic field between flat, opposite poles of magnets can be represented this way.

questions.explanation: The magnetic field lines form concentric circles around the wire. If you wrap your right hand’s fingers around the wire with your thumb pointing in the direction of the current, then the direction in which the fingers curl will give the direction of the magnetic field. As the current is directed out of the page, then the magnetic field will have a counter clockwise direction. So “D” is the correct answer.

questions.text: Two parallel long wires carry the same current and repel each other with a force F per unit length. If both these currents are doubled and the wire separation tripled, the force per unit length becomes:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect as per calculations.
Incorrect as per calculations.
Incorrect as per calculations.

When the two parallel long wires carry the same current and repel each other with a force F per unit length we have:F₁ / ℓ = I² B μ₀ / (2 π h)And when the currents are doubled and the wire separation tripled, the force per unit length becomes:F₂ / ℓ = (2I)² B μ₀ / (2 π (3h))F₂ / ℓ = 4 I² B μ₀ / (2 π (3h))F₂ / ℓ = (4/3) I² B μ₀ / (2 π h) = (4/3) F₁ / ℓTherefore, when the currents are doubled and the wire separation tripled, the force per unit length becomes 4/3 of the initial force per unit length

questions.explanation: When the two parallel long wires carry the same current and repel each other with a force F per unit length we have:F₁ / ℓ = I² B μ₀ / (2 π h)And when the currents are doubled and the wire separation tripled, the force per unit length becomes:F₂ / ℓ = (2I)² B μ₀ / (2 π (3h))F₂ / ℓ = 4 I² B μ₀ / (2 π (3h))F₂ / ℓ = (4/3) I² B μ₀ / (2 π h) = (4/3) F₁ / ℓTherefore, when the currents are doubled and the wire separation tripled, the force per unit length becomes 4/3 of the initial force per unit length.

questions.text: Moving charges have:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Electric field and magnetic field both
options.explanation: A moving charge generates an electric field due to its charge and a magnetic field due to its motion. Thus, it has both fields.
This is incorrect because moving charges do produce fields. A moving charge cannot have no fields at all.
This is incorrect because, while a moving charge does produce a magnetic field, it also inherently has an electric field due to its charge.
This is incorrect because a moving charge generates a magnetic field in addition to its electric field.

questions.explanation: A moving charge generates both an electric field and a magnetic field. The electric field is due to the charge itself, as any charged particle will create an electric field in its surroundings. When the charge is in motion, it also generates a magnetic field, as moving charges are a source of magnetic fields according to electromagnetic theory. Therefore, the correct answer is that moving charges have both electric and magnetic fields.

questions.text: In a certain region, magnetic field is directed vertically downward. If a plane flies due South, which part of the plane will be negatively charged?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The front part of the plane moves forward in the direction of travel (South), but the right-hand rule shows that it does not experience a negative charge.
The back part also moves South and does not experience the force that would result in it being negatively charged.
The left wingtip is not in the direction of the force experienced by negative charges according to the right-hand rule.
The right wingtip is where the negative charge accumulates, as determined by the right-hand rule: the thumb points East, indicating that electrons move toward the West side of the plane.

questions.explanation: To determine which part of the plane will be negatively charged, we apply the right-hand rule. Point your fingers in the direction of the plane's velocity (South) and curl them toward the direction of the magnetic field (downward). Your thumb will then point toward the East, indicating the direction of the force acting on positive charges.Since electrons are negatively charged, they will be pushed in the opposite direction (West). Therefore, the right wingtip of the plane will accumulate negative charge due to this force.Other options are incorrect because the front, back, and left wingtip of the plane do not align with the force direction affecting negative charges, which ultimately leads to the right wingtip being the only area where a negative charge accumulates.

questions.text: Time-varying magnetic field creates an electric field, this is called _.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Electric induction: This is not the correct term used for the phenomenon. The correct term is electromagnetic induction.
Magnetic induction: This term is not the correct one either. The correct answer is electromagnetic induction.

The correct answer is "electromagnetic induction". It is a phenomenon in which a time-varying magnetic field generates an electric field and vice versa. This is the basis for the operation of many electrical devices, including transformers, generators, and motors.

Dipole induction: This term is unrelated to the time-varying magnetic fields creating electric fields. It generally refers to the induction of electric dipoles in molecules due to an external electric field.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct since electromagnetic induction is a process in which a conductor is put in a particular position and the magnetic field keeps varying or the magnetic field is stationary and a conductor is moving. This produces a Voltage or EMF (Electromotive Force) across the electrical conductor.

questions.text: If compass needles are placed on a card board along a circle, with the center at the wire, they will point in:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Compass needles point in the N-S direction because the Earth itself is a magnet. The Earth's magnetic field is strongest at the north and south poles, and weakest at the equator. The compass needle is a magnet, and it aligns itself with the Earth's magnetic field. The north pole of the compass needle points towards the south magnetic pole of the Earth, and the south pole of the compass needle points towards the north magnetic pole of the Earth.
The Earth's magnetic field is not aligned with the east-west direction.
The Earth's magnetic field is not aligned with the north-east direction.
The Earth's magnetic field is not aligned with the west-south direction.

questions.explanation: The magnetic compass will point in N-S direction.Compass needles point in the N-S direction because the Earth itself is a magnet. The Earth's magnetic field is strongest at the north and south poles, and weakest at the equator. The compass needle is a magnet, and it aligns itself with the Earth's magnetic field. The north pole of the compass needle points towards the south magnetic pole of the Earth, and the south pole of the compass needle points towards the north magnetic pole of the Earth.

questions.text: A conductor carrying a current 'I' has length 'L'. When it is placed in a magnetic field 'B' at 90°, it experiences a force:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is correct. The force on a conductor carrying current 'I' in a magnetic field 'B' at an angle of 90° is given by the formula F = BILsin(θ). Since the angle is 90°, sin(90°) = 1, making the force BIL.
This is incorrect. The formula for force does not involve squaring the magnetic field 'B'. The correct formula is F = BILsin(θ), not B²LI.
This is incorrect. The current 'I' should not be squared in the formula for the force. The correct formula is F = BILsin(θ), not BLI².
This is incorrect. The force is zero if the angle is 0° or 180°, but here the angle is 90°, which results in a maximum force.
This is incorrect. The force is finite and given by the formula F = BILsin(θ). It cannot be infinite.

questions.explanation: The formula for the force experienced by a current-carrying conductor is BILsin(θ), where theta represents the angle between the current, I, and the magnetic field, B, hence sin(90)=1 and the value of the force will be BIL (maximum).

questions.text: Due to the current in a straight conductor the difference between magnetic field lines:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When an electric current flows through a straight conductor, it generates a magnetic field around the conductor. The magnetic field lines form concentric circles around the conductor, and as you move away from the conductor, the density of these magnetic field lines increases. This means that the number of field lines per unit area increases as you move further away from the conductor. Therefore, the difference between magnetic field lines increases away from the conductor.

This option is incorrect. As explained above, the density of magnetic field lines increases as you move away from the conductor, resulting in an increase in the difference between magnetic field lines, not a decrease.
This option is incorrect. The density of magnetic field lines decreases as you move towards the conductor, resulting in a decrease in the difference between magnetic field lines, not an increase.

options.text: Decreases and then increases towards conductor
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The magnetic field lines form concentric circles around the conductor, and as you move towards the conductor, the density of these magnetic field lines increases, leading to an increase in the difference between magnetic field lines, not a decrease followed by an increase.

questions.text: The force exerted on a wire of one meter length carrying one ampere current placed at right angle to the field is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: It is the force when a charge experience both electric and magnetic field.

Self-inductance refers to the inherent property of a conductor or a circuit to induce an electromotive force (emf) in itself when the amount of current flowing through it changes. It occurs due to the magnetic field produced by the changing current within the conductor or coil.

Magnetic flux is a measure of the amount of magnetic field penetrating a given surface. It is a fundamental concept in electromagnetism and is defined as the product of the magnetic field strength and the area it penetrates.

Magnetic induction, also known as electromagnetic induction, refers to the process by which a changing magnetic field induces an electric current in a nearby conductor. This fundamental principle in physics was discovered by Michael Faraday in the early 19th century.

questions.explanation: Magnetic induction, also known as electromagnetic induction, refers to the process by which a changing magnetic field induces an electric current in a nearby conductor. This fundamental principle in physics was discovered by Michael Faraday in the early 19th century.

questions.text: If the direction of current in the conductor and the magnetic field are the same, in which way will the conductor move?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Fleming's left-hand rule states that when the current and magnetic field are in the same direction, no force acts on the conductor, so it remains stationary.

options.text: At an acute angle with direction of current.
options.explanation: This is incorrect because the force would need to be perpendicular to create movement, not at an acute angle.
Incorrect, as the force is perpendicular only when the current and magnetic field are at right angles, not when they are parallel.
This is incorrect as the force on the conductor does not act along the direction of the current.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is A: It will not move.Fleming's left-hand rule helps determine the direction of force on a current-carrying conductor within a magnetic field. When the magnetic field and current direction are the same, the rule shows no force is applied, and hence, no movement occurs. Option A is correct as the conductor will not experience any force.Option B is incorrect because a force at an acute angle requires the magnetic field and current to intersect at an angle, which is not the case here.Option C is incorrect because the force is perpendicular only when the magnetic field and current are not parallel.Option D is incorrect because the force does not act in the direction of the current when the current and magnetic field align.

questions.text: Force on the current carrying conductor per unit length is given by:

options.explanation: IL sinϴ: This option suggests that the force on a current-carrying conductor per unit length is given by IL sinϴ. However, this expression is incorrect.

ILB: This option suggests that the force on a current-carrying conductor per unit length is given by ILB. This expression is also incorrect. The correct expression includes the sinϴ term, representing the component of the magnetic field perpendicular to the current direction.

IL: This option suggests that the force on a current-carrying conductor per unit length is given by IL. However, this expression is incorrect. The force on a current-carrying conductor is dependent on both the current and the magnetic field, and the expression should include both terms.

IB sinϴ: This option suggests that the force on a current-carrying conductor per unit length is given by IB sinϴ. This is the correct expression. The force on a current-carrying conductor is proportional to the product of the current (I), the magnetic field strength (B), and the sine of the angle (ϴ) between the current direction and the magnetic field direction.

questions.explanation: IB sinϴ: This option suggests that the force on a current-carrying conductor per unit length is given by IB sinϴ. This is the correct expression. The force on a current-carrying conductor is proportional to the product of the current (I), the magnetic field strength (B), and the sine of the angle (ϴ) between the current direction and the magnetic field direction.

questions.text: Magnetic field is set up in a region around a conductor, carrying:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When charges are in motion, they have current (AC, DC or pulsating DC); magnetic field is produced around a current carrying conductor due to the flow of these charges.
When charges are in motion, they have current (AC, DC or pulsating DC); magnetic field is produced around a current carrying conductor due to the flow of these charge.
When charges are in motion, they have current (AC, DC or pulsating DC); magnetic field is produced around a current carrying conductor due to the flow of these charge.

questions.explanation: When charges are in motion, they have current (AC, DC or pulsating DC); magnetic field is produced around a current carrying conductor due to the flow of these charge.andMagnetic field is created whenever an electric current flows through a conductor. This is because a moving electrical charge creates a magnetic field. The strength and direction of the magnetic field depend on the amount of current flowing and the direction of flow. Magnetic fields can also be created by permanent magnets (bar magnets). These are made from materials that are naturally good conductors of electricity, such as iron. The molecules in these materials line up in rows, which creates a strong magnetic field. When you bring a conductor close to a magnet, the magnetic field from the magnet affects the moving charges in the conductor. This causes current flow, creating a current-carrying conductor, which creates its own magnetic field. These two fields interact with each other, resulting in a force that either attracts or repels the objects.

As per the calculations (refer to the explanation below), the correct answer is F = 3/√2. Hence, this option is incorrect.

Using the formula F = BIL sinθ Where, B = Magnetic field strength = 0.15T I = Current = 10A L = Length of the conductor = 2m θ = Angle between the conductor and magnetic field = 45° F = 0.15 x 10 x 2 x sin45 (substituing sin45 = 1/√2) F = 1.5 x 2 x 1/√2 F = 3 x 1/√2 F = 3/√2

questions.explanation: Using the formula F = BIL sinθ Where, B = Magnetic field strength = 0.15T I = Current = 10A L = Length of the conductor = 2m θ = Angle between the conductor and magnetic field = 45° F = 0.15 x 10 x 2 x sin45 (substituing sin45 = 1/√2) F = 1.5 x 2 x 1/√2 F = 3 x 1/√2 F = 3/√2 You can use the following table to memorize important values for sin cos and tan to boost your calculation speed!

questions.text: Find the value of the magnetic field that will cause a maximum force of 7.0 x 10-3N on a 20.0 cm straight wire carrying a current of 10.0 A.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Formula:F = BIL sinθWhere:F is the force on the wire (in newtons)B is the magnetic field (in teslas)I is the current in the wire (in amperes)L is the length of the wire in the magnetic field (in meters)θ is the angle between the wire and the magnetic field directionFor maximum force, θ = 90°, so sinθ = 1Given:F = 7.0 × 10⁻³ NL = 20.0 cm = 0.200 mI = 10.0 Aθ = 90°, so sinθ = 1Plug into the formula:F = BILSo,B = F / (I × L)B = (7.0 × 10⁻³) / (10.0 × 0.200)B = (7.0 × 10⁻³) / 2.0B = 3.5 × 10⁻³ TFinal Answer:B = 3.5 × 10⁻³ tesla or 3.5 mT (millitesla)
This is not correct as per explanation and calculation.

This is not correct as per explanation and calculation.

questions.explanation: Formula:F = BIL sinθWhere:F is the force on the wire (in newtons)B is the magnetic field (in teslas)I is the current in the wire (in amperes)L is the length of the wire in the magnetic field (in meters)θ is the angle between the wire and the magnetic field directionFor maximum force, θ = 90°, so sinθ = 1Given:F = 7.0 × 10⁻³ NL = 20.0 cm = 0.200 mI = 10.0 Aθ = 90°, so sinθ = 1Plug into the formula:F = BILSo,B = F / (I × L) B = (7.0 × 10⁻³) / (10.0 × 0.200) B = (7.0 × 10⁻³) / 2.0 B = 3.5 × 10⁻³ T Final Answer:B = 3.5 × 10⁻³ tesla or 3.5 mT (millitesla)

questions.text: In F = ILB sin θ, θ is the angle between:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The angle θ is not between the force F and the current I. The formula relates θ to the orientation of the conductor and the magnetic field, not the force and current.
The angle θ does not pertain to the force F and the length L of the conductor. The formula uses θ to measure the relative orientation of the conductor and the magnetic field.
Correct. The angle θ in the formula F = ILB sin θ represents the angle between the direction of the length L of the conductor and the magnetic field B.
The angle θ is not defined between the current I and the length L of the conductor in this context. It is concerned with the orientation of the conductor relative to the magnetic field B.

questions.explanation: The formula F = ILB sin θ describes the magnetic force on a current-carrying conductor in a uniform magnetic field. Here, θ is the angle between the direction of the conductor (L) and the magnetic field (B). When L is perpendicular to B (θ = 90°), the sin 90° = 1, resulting in maximum force: F = ILB. If L is parallel to B (θ = 0°), then sin 0° = 0, and the force is zero: F = 0.Option A is incorrect because θ is not the angle between the force and the current. Option B is incorrect because θ is not the angle between the force and the length of the conductor. Option D is incorrect because θ is not the angle between the current and the length of the conductor.

options.explanation: Incorrect. Magnetic flux lines are invisible without tools like iron filings.
Incorrect. Microscopes are for microscopic objects, not magnetic fields.
Incorrect. Telescopes are for distant objects, not magnetic fields.

Magnetic flux lines are invisible to the naked eye but can be visualized indirectly using tools like iron filings or magnetic field sensors, which reveal the field’s pattern. None of the provided options accurately describe this process, as direct visualization, microscopes, or telescopes are not suitable. Since none or a similar option is not explicitly listed, I assume All cases are not possible is closest, implying no direct visualization method among the choices. This is relevant for medical students studying physics in applications like MRI.

All cases are not possible is the closest correct answer, as magnetic flux lines require indirect visualization, not direct sight, microscopes, or telescopes. questions.text: Magnetic induction is also called _.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Flux is a measure of the number of electric or magnetic field lines passing through a surface in a given amount of time. Field lines provide a mechanism for visualizing the magnitude and direction of the field being measured.
Magnetization, also termed magnetic polarization, is a vector quantity that measures the density of permanent or induced dipole moment in a given magnetic material.
Magnetic flux density or magnetic induction is the number of lines of force passing through a unit area of material, B. The unit of magnetic induction is the tesla (T).

Flux is the rate at which a quantity goes through a fixed boundary per unit time or flux is the surface integral of a field at a given time. Intensity is defined as the energy flux according to the first definition (of flux) E/A or (A*t).

questions.explanation: Correct option is C.The magnetic flux density or magnetic induction is also known is magnetic field strength , B. The unit of magnetic induction is the tesla (T).

questions.text: The strength of a magnetic field is called?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Strength is not correct because it is a general term and does not specifically refer to the strength of a magnetic field.
Flux is not correct because it refers to the total amount of magnetic field passing through a given area, which depends on both the strength and the size of the magnetic field.

Magnetic flux density (B) is defined as the force acting per unit current per unit length on a wire placed at right angles to the magnetic field. Magnetic field strength is also called Magnetic Intensity or Magnetic Field Intensity or Magnetic Field Strength.
Density is not correct because it is a general term that refers to the amount of a substance or quantity per unit volume or area, and does not specifically refer to the strength of a magnetic field.

questions.explanation: .Option A) Strength is not correct because it is a general term and does not specifically refer to the strength of a magnetic field.Option B) Flux is not correct because it refers to the total amount of magnetic field passing through a given area, which depends on both the strength and the size of the magnetic field.Option C) Magnetic flux density (B) is defined as the force acting per unit current per unit length on a wire placed at right angles to the magnetic field. Magnetic field strength is also called Magnetic Intensity or Magnetic Field Intensity or Magnetic Field Strength. Option D) Density is not correct because it is a general term that refers to the amount of a substance or quantity per unit volume or area and does not specifically refer to the strength of a magnetic field.

questions.text: The direction of magnetic field at a point on the magnetic lines of force can be taken along:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The normal to the magnetic field at a point is perpendicular to the magnetic field at that point. However, the magnetic field lines are tangent to the magnetic field at each point, so the normal to the magnetic field is not the same as the tangent to the magnetic field lines.

options.text: Axis of the magnetic line of force at that point
options.explanation: The axis of the magnetic line of force is the line along which the magnetic field lines are oriented. However, the axis of the magnetic line of force is not necessarily perpendicular to the magnetic field at that point.

The direction of magnetic field at a point on the magnetic lines of force can be taken along the tangent at that point. This is because the magnetic field lines are tangent to the magnetic field at each point. The normal to the magnetic field at a point is perpendicular to the magnetic field at that point, and the axis of the magnetic line of force is not necessarily perpendicular to the magnetic field at that point.The direction of the magnetic field at a point can also be determined using the right-hand rule. If you point your right thumb in the direction of the current, and your fingers curl in the direction of the magnetic field, your palm will be pointing in the direction of the magnetic force.

options.text: Perpendicular to the axis of the solenoid
options.explanation: The axis of a solenoid is the central axis of the solenoid. The magnetic field inside a solenoid is parallel to the axis of the solenoid, so the direction of the magnetic field at a point on the magnetic lines of force is not necessarily perpendicular to the axis of the solenoid.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C. The direction of the magnetic field at a point on magnetic lines of force is along the tangent to the field line at that point. This is because magnetic field lines represent the path along which a north pole would move, and at any given point, this motion is tangential to the curve of the field line.Option A is incorrect because the normal to a point is perpendicular to the field line and does not indicate the direction of the magnetic field.Option B is incorrect because the axis of the magnetic line of force is a conceptual line and does not necessarily indicate the direction of the magnetic field at every point.Option D is incorrect because the magnetic field within a solenoid is parallel to its axis, not perpendicular.

questions.text: Magnetic field lines have a property that lines are _.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option suggests that magnetic field lines do not intersect each other. In reality, magnetic field lines do not intersect because they represent the path of the magnetic field around a magnetic object. Magnetic field lines always form closed loops, and they do not cross each other.

This option suggests that magnetic field lines intersect near the south pole of a magnet. However, this is not correct. Magnetic field lines emerge from the north pole of a magnet and re-enter at the south pole, forming closed loops. They do not intersect each other at the south pole or anywhere else.

This option suggests that magnetic field lines intersect near the north pole of a magnet. Similar to option (b), this is not correct. Magnetic field lines do not intersect each other at the north pole or anywhere else. Instead, they emerge from the north pole and re-enter at the south pole.

This option suggests that magnetic field lines intersect at every point around a magnet. However, this is not correct. Magnetic field lines never intersect each other. They always form closed loops, and they do not cross.

questions.explanation: The magnetic field lines do not intersect, under normal conditions, without any external influences.

questions.text: the magnitude of force on a moving charge is not zero when the angle between the velocity of the charge carrier and the magnetic field is zero. The magnetic force on a charged particle is given by the following equation: F = qvB sin θ where: F is the magnetic force (N) q is the charge of the particle (C) v is the velocity of the particle (m/s) B is the magnetic field strength (T) θ is the angle between the direction of the velocity and the direction of the magnetic field (°) When the angle between the velocity and the magnetic field is zero, then the sine of the angle is also zero. sin 0° = 0 The magnetic force is then zero. Therefore, the magnitude of the force on a moving charge is not zero when the angle between the velocity of the charge carrier and the magnetic field is zero.

questions.text: When two straight current carrying conductors are placed parallel near each other, their fields will_in the middle region:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If the current flows in straight parallel wires in same direction, then the megnetic field in their middle region tend to cancel each other (attraction).
If the current flows in straight parallel wires in opposite direction, then the magnetic field in their middle region will reinforce each other (repulsion).
They will indeed affect each other so this is wrong.
As both these options are correct so this is also correct.

questions.explanation: If the current flows in straight parallel wires in same direction, then the megnetic field in their middle region tend to cancel each other (attraction). if the current flows in straight parallel wires in opposite direction, then the magnetic field in their middle region will reinforce each other (repulsion).Two Current Carrying ConductorsWhen two wires carrying a current are placed parallel to each other, their magnetic fields will interact, resulting in a force acting between the wires. The magnitude of the force acting on each wire is equal, but the directions are opposite. This is true even if the conductors carry currents of different magnitudes.The diagram below illustrates two examples where the direction of the magnetic field around each wire is drawn with the • × notation. Diagram A shows the current in the wires travelling in the same direction and diagram B shows the current travelling in opposite directions:
